enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium - free WordPress.org plugin plus paid Pro, Business, and Agency subscription tiers

enrichment.seo_title: Rank Math Review 2026 - Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rank Math is a WordPress SEO plugin with schema markup, rank tracking, and AI content tools. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons before you buy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rank Math is a WordPress search engine optimization plugin first launched in 2018 by a development team associated with MyThemeShop and headquartered in New Delhi, India. The company operates as a fully remote team of roughly two dozen people and has grown into one of the most widely installed SEO plugins in the WordPress ecosystem, competing directly with Yoast SEO and All in One SEO. Rank Math follows a freemium business model: a free version is distributed through the official WordPress.org plugin repository and includes on-page SEO analysis, Google Search Console integration inside the WordPress dashboard, XML sitemap generation, and support for 18 predefined schema types for rich snippets. Paid Rank Math Pro plans unlock additional capabilities such as an Advanced Rank Tracker, a broken link checker, automated internal keyword linking, a free trial of the separate Content AI writing assistant, and support for more websites and tracked keywords. Pricing is tiered by use case: the Pro plan targets individual bloggers and solopreneurs with unlimited personal websites and tracking for 500 keywords; the Business plan is aimed at freelancers and small agencies, supporting around 100 client websites and 10,000 tracked keywords; and the Agency plan scales to roughly 500 client websites and 50,000 tracked keywords with priority support. All paid plans are billed annually with a stated discount off the regular renewal rate, and the company offers a 30-day money-back guarantee. Core functionality includes a real-time SEO score and content analysis as content is written, structured data (schema) generation for articles, products, FAQs, recipes, local businesses, and more, redirection and 404 monitoring tools, a dedicated WooCommerce SEO module for online stores, and a Local SEO module for businesses managing Google Business Profile-style structured data. Rank Math also provides a built-in importer that can migrate settings and metadata directly from competing plugins such as Yoast SEO and All in One SEO, which lowers the switching cost for site owners evaluating alternatives. Because it runs entirely inside the WordPress admin dashboard, Rank Math does not require a separate SaaS login for its core SEO features, though the optional Content AI writing tool operates on its own credit-based subscription. The plugin is best suited to WordPress site owners, bloggers, ecommerce merchants using WooCommerce, and small-to-midsize agencies managing multiple client sites who want a single tool covering technical SEO, structured data, and keyword tracking without stitching together several separate plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rank Math is a WordPress SEO plugin built to give site owners granular control over technical and on-page optimization directly from the WordPress admin dashboard. Since its 2018 launch, it has positioned itself as a feature-rich free alternative to established plugins like Yoast SEO, bundling schema markup, sitemaps, and Search Console reporting into the free tier.

The plugin is developed by a remote team based in New Delhi, India, and is distributed both through the official WordPress.org repository and directly from rankmath.com for the paid Pro tiers. Its setup wizard is designed to configure most technical SEO settings automatically, which the company markets as reducing the learning curve compared to more manual competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rank Math's schema generator supports 18 or more predefined structured data types out of the box in the free version, letting site owners mark up articles, products, recipes, FAQs, and local business information without writing JSON-LD by hand. The plugin also includes a real-time content analysis score that checks a post against dozens of on-page SEO factors as it is written.

Paid plans add an Advanced Rank Tracker for monitoring keyword positions, a broken link checker, automated internal keyword linking, and priority support. A dedicated WooCommerce SEO module and Local SEO module extend the plugin for ecommerce stores and location-based businesses, while a built-in importer migrates settings from Yoast SEO and other competing plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rank Math uses a freemium model. The free plugin, available on WordPress.org, covers core on-page SEO, sitemaps, Search Console integration, and basic schema support for unlimited sites. Paid Pro plans are billed annually and scale by number of managed websites and tracked keywords.

The entry-level Pro plan is aimed at individual site owners with unlimited personal websites and 500 tracked keywords, the Business plan targets agencies with around 100 client sites and 10,000 tracked keywords, and the Agency plan extends to roughly 500 sites and 50,000 tracked keywords. A separate Content AI add-on for AI-assisted writing is sold on its own credit-based plans.

features.title: Schema and rich snippet generator

features.title: Real-time SEO content analysis

features.title: Automated XML sitemaps
features.description: Generates and maintains SEO sitemaps automatically as content is published or updated.

features.title: WooCommerce SEO module
features.description: Dedicated optimization tools for product pages, categories, and ecommerce schema.

features.title: 404 monitor and redirections
features.description: Tracks broken links and lets site owners set up redirects without a separate plugin.

features.title: One-click plugin importer
features.description: Migrates settings and metadata from Yoast SEO, All in One SEO, and other competing plugins.

pros: Generous free tier compared to many competing WordPress SEO plugins
Built-in schema generator covering many structured data types without add-ons
Google Search Console data available inside the WordPress dashboard
Rank tracking and broken-link checking included in paid tiers
Easy migration from Yoast SEO and other plugins via built-in importer
cons: Interface and setup wizard can feel dense for complete SEO beginners
Rank tracking and several advanced features require a paid Pro plan
Can occasionally conflict with certain caching or other SEO plugins
Content AI writing add-on is a separate subscription, not included in core pricing

faqs.question: Is Rank Math free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rank Math offers a free version on WordPress.org with on-page SEO analysis, sitemaps, Search Console integration, and basic schema support. Pro plans add keyword rank tracking, more schema options, and priority support.

faqs.question: What is Rank Math used for?
faqs.answer: Rank Math is a WordPress plugin used for on-page SEO optimization, including meta tag management, XML sitemaps, structured data/schema markup, redirects, and keyword rank tracking.

faqs.question: Does Rank Math work with WooCommerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rank Math includes a dedicated WooCommerce SEO module for optimizing product pages and store structured data.

faqs.question: Can I import my settings from Yoast SEO?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rank Math has a built-in importer that migrates settings and metadata from Yoast SEO, All in One SEO, and several other plugins.

faqs.question: How many keywords can Rank Math track?
faqs.answer: Keyword rank tracking is a Pro feature. The entry-level Pro plan tracks around 500 keywords, scaling up to roughly 50,000 keywords on the Agency plan.

faqs.question: Who develops Rank Math?
faqs.answer: Rank Math is developed by a remote team headquartered in New Delhi, India, and was first launched in 2018.

faqs.question: Is Rank Math good for local SEO?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rank Math includes a Local SEO module for managing location-based structured data and business information.

faqs.question: Does Rank Math offer a money-back guarantee?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rank Math advertises a 30-day money-back guarantee on its paid Pro plans.

use_cases.title: Blog and content site optimization
use_cases.description: Solo bloggers and content creators use Rank Math's free and Pro tiers to manage on-page SEO, schema, and sitemaps for a single site.

use_cases.title: Multi-site agency SEO management
use_cases.description: Agencies use the Business and Agency plans to manage SEO settings, schema, and rank tracking across dozens or hundreds of client WordPress sites from one license.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce product SEO
use_cases.description: WooCommerce store owners use Rank Math's dedicated ecommerce module to optimize product and category pages and structured data for shopping results.

enrichment.seo_title: Rapid7 Review 2026 - Products, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rapid7 is a Boston-based cybersecurity company offering vulnerability management, SIEM/XDR, and managed detection and response. See products, pricing notes, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rapid7 is a publicly traded cybersecurity company founded in Boston, Massachusetts in July 2000 by Alan Matthews, Tas Giakouminakis, and Chad Loder. The company trades on NASDAQ under the ticker RPD and remains headquartered in Boston, with employee counts reported in the 2,500 to 3,300 range depending on the source and reporting period. Rapid7 describes itself as a global leader in AI-powered managed cybersecurity operations, and its unifying product is the Rapid7 Command Platform, which integrates security telemetry across an organization's attack surface, enriches it with threat intelligence and AI, and feeds it into detection, response, and exposure management workflows. The company's product portfolio spans several major categories. On the exposure and vulnerability management side, Rapid7 offers InsightVM and the legacy Nexpose product line for identifying, prioritizing, and remediating vulnerabilities across on-premises and cloud assets, along with Attack Surface Management (ASM) capabilities for continuously discovering internet-facing exposure. On the detection and response side, InsightIDR functions as a next-generation SIEM and XDR platform, correlating logs, endpoint data, and network telemetry to detect threats, and Rapid7 layers a Managed Detection and Response (MDR) service on top, providing a 24/7 security operations center staffed by Rapid7 analysts for organizations that want outsourced monitoring and response. Cloud-native application protection is handled by InsightCloudSec, and application security testing is covered by InsightAppSec, a dynamic application security testing (DAST) tool. Rapid7 also maintains a Threat Intelligence offering and application security consulting, penetration testing, and IoT security testing services. The company is closely associated with two widely known open-source security projects: Metasploit, the penetration testing framework used broadly across the offensive security community, and Velociraptor, an open-source endpoint monitoring and digital forensics tool, both of which Rapid7 maintains and uses to build credibility and community goodwill among security practitioners. Rapid7 reports serving more than 11,500 customers worldwide, ranging from mid-market businesses to large enterprises, and positions itself against competitors such as Tenable, Qualys, CrowdStrike, and Splunk depending on the specific product category. Because Rapid7 sells to enterprise and mid-market security teams, pricing is not published on its website and is instead quoted individually based on the number of assets, users, or the specific combination of Insight Platform modules an organization licenses; prospective customers typically need to request a demo or quote directly from Rapid7's sales team to get pricing. The platform is best suited for IT and security teams that need a single vendor covering vulnerability management, detection and response, and cloud security, or organizations that want to outsource day-to-day security monitoring to a managed SOC rather than staffing one internally.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rapid7 is a Boston-founded cybersecurity company that has operated since 2000 and now trades publicly on NASDAQ under the ticker RPD. The company built its reputation on vulnerability management before expanding into a broader security operations platform covering detection and response, cloud security, and application security.

Rapid7's central product is the Rapid7 Command Platform, which unifies data from across an organization's attack surface and applies AI and threat intelligence to help security teams prioritize risk and respond to incidents faster. The company also maintains widely used open-source security tools, including the Metasploit penetration testing framework and the Velociraptor forensics tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rapid7's product line spans exposure management (InsightVM, Attack Surface Management), detection and response (InsightIDR next-generation SIEM/XDR, Managed Detection and Response), cloud security (InsightCloudSec), and application security (InsightAppSec DAST testing). Each module can be adopted individually or combined under the broader Command Platform.

Beyond software, Rapid7 offers professional and managed services, including a 24/7 managed detection and response SOC, penetration testing, security advisory consulting, and IoT security testing, aimed at organizations that need hands-on expertise in addition to tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rapid7 does not publish self-serve pricing on its website. As an enterprise security vendor, pricing is quote-based and typically scales with the number of monitored assets, users, or the combination of Insight Platform products an organization licenses.

Prospective buyers generally need to request a demo or quote directly from Rapid7's sales team, and pricing can vary substantially depending on deployment size, whether managed services like MDR are included, and contract length.

faqs.answer: Yes, Rapid7 is publicly traded on NASDAQ under the ticker symbol RPD.

faqs.answer: InsightIDR is Rapid7's cloud SIEM and extended detection and response (XDR) product for threat detection and investigation.

faqs.answer: Metasploit is a widely used open-source penetration testing framework maintained by Rapid7.

faqs.question: How many customers does Rapid7 have?
faqs.answer: Rapid7 reports serving more than 11,500 customers worldwide.

faqs.question: Where is Rapid7 headquartered?
faqs.answer: Rapid7 is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, United States.

enrichment.seo_title: Rask AI Review 2026 - Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rask AI is an AI video and audio localization platform offering dubbing, lip-sync, and voice cloning in 130+ languages. See 2026 pricing, features, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rask AI is an AI-powered video and audio localization platform founded in 2023 by Maria Chmir, built to help creators and businesses translate and dub content into more than 130 languages without traditional dubbing studios. The company is registered in Dover, Delaware, though its team operates in a distributed fashion, and public reporting describes it as a lean startup team generating multi-million dollar revenue with roughly two dozen employees as of 2025. Rask AI's core product takes an uploaded video or audio file, transcribes the source speech with automated speech-to-text, translates it into the target language, and generates dubbed audio using AI voice cloning technology that can preserve elements of the original speaker's voice identity. A key differentiator is lip-sync technology, which adjusts the appearance of mouth movement in the video to better match the translated audio track, aiming for a more natural viewing experience than traditional dubbing where lip movement stays out of sync with the new language. The platform includes a built-in video editor for making adjustments after translation, team collaboration and project management tools for agencies or in-house localization teams working on multiple videos at once, and controls for maintaining consistent brand terminology across a catalog of translated content. Rask AI's pricing is structured as minute-based subscription tiers. A free trial allows translation of up to three videos capped at one minute each, giving prospective customers a way to sample dubbing quality before subscribing. The paid tiers are Creator (aimed at individual creators, offering a set number of minutes per year with annual billing discounts of up to roughly 45 percent versus monthly billing), Creator Pro (adding translation accuracy controls, lip-sync with speaker identity preservation, and team collaboration features), Business (aimed at teams needing batch video processing and brand consistency tooling at higher volume), and a custom Enterprise plan that adds security and compliance readiness, service-level agreements, custom API integrations, and managed quality assurance for large-scale or regulated deployments. On annual plans, unused minutes roll over, while additional minutes beyond a plan's allocation on the Business tier are billed at a per-minute overage rate. Rask AI is positioned as a tool for content creators and YouTubers expanding into international audiences, marketing teams localizing ad and product videos for global campaigns, and corporate training or e-learning teams that need to dub instructional content into multiple languages efficiently. Because pricing scales with translated minutes rather than seats, the platform is most cost-effective for teams with a predictable, moderate volume of video content, while very high-volume or highly regulated use cases are typically directed toward the custom Enterprise tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rask AI is a video and audio localization platform founded in 2023 by Maria Chmir, designed to let creators and businesses dub and translate content into more than 130 languages using AI rather than traditional dubbing studios. The company is registered in Dover, Delaware, with a distributed team.

The platform automates the traditionally manual dubbing pipeline: transcription, translation, voice generation, and (on higher tiers) lip-sync adjustment, compressing a process that could take days in a studio into a workflow that runs largely automatically after a video is uploaded.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rask AI's core capability is AI dubbing with optional lip-sync, paired with voice cloning that can preserve elements of the original speaker's voice across more than 30 languages. Automated speech-to-text transcription and multi-language subtitle generation support both dubbing and captioning workflows from the same source file.

Higher tiers add team collaboration and project management for organizations handling many videos at once, controls for keeping brand and terminology consistent across a translated content library, and batch processing. The Enterprise tier layers on API access, security and compliance readiness, and managed quality assurance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rask AI prices its subscriptions by minutes of translated video per year, with tiers named Creator, Creator Pro, Business, and Enterprise. Annual billing is discounted substantially compared to paying monthly, and unused minutes on annual plans roll over.

A free trial allows up to three one-minute video translations. Business-tier customers who exceed their included minute allocation are billed extra per minute, while Enterprise pricing and minute allocation are negotiated individually.

features.title: Automated transcription
features.description: Converts source video or audio speech into text automatically as the first step of the localization pipeline.

features.title: Multi-language subtitles
features.description: Generates translated subtitle files alongside or instead of full audio dubbing.

features.title: Built-in video editor
features.description: Allows post-translation adjustments to the video without leaving the platform.

features.title: Team collaboration tools
features.description: Project management features for teams coordinating localization across many videos.

features.title: Brand and terminology consistency
features.description: Controls to keep specific terms and brand names consistent across a catalog of translated videos.

features.title: API and enterprise integrations
features.description: Custom API access, SLAs, and managed QA available on the Enterprise plan.

pros: Wide language coverage with 130+ supported languages for translation and dubbing
Lip-sync technology aims for more natural-looking dubbed video than traditional methods
Voice cloning helps preserve original speaker identity across languages
Significantly faster turnaround than traditional studio dubbing workflows
Annual billing offers substantial discounts versus monthly pricing
cons: Business and Enterprise tiers are priced well above typical creator budgets
Per-minute overage charges can add up quickly for high-volume users
Free trial is limited to three one-minute videos, making it hard to fully evaluate quality upfront
Dubbing and voice cloning quality can vary by specific language pair

faqs.question: What is Rask AI used for?
faqs.answer: Rask AI is used to translate and dub video and audio content into more than 130 languages, using AI transcription, translation, voice cloning, and lip-sync.

faqs.question: Who founded Rask AI?
faqs.answer: Rask AI was founded in 2023 by Maria Chmir.

faqs.question: Does Rask AI offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, the free trial allows translation of up to three videos, each capped at one minute.

faqs.question: How does Rask AI pricing work?
faqs.answer: Rask AI uses minute-based subscription tiers (Creator, Creator Pro, Business, Enterprise), with a set number of translated minutes included per year and discounted annual billing.

faqs.question: Does Rask AI support voice cloning?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rask AI offers voice cloning that preserves elements of the original speaker's identity across 30+ languages.

faqs.question: Can Rask AI sync dubbed audio to lip movement?
faqs.answer: Yes, lip-sync technology is available starting on the Creator Pro plan.

faqs.question: What happens to unused minutes on Rask AI?
faqs.answer: On annual plans, unused minutes roll over; on the Business plan, extra minutes beyond the included allocation are billed at $3 per minute.

faqs.question: Is Rask AI suitable for enterprise use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise plan offers custom minute allocations, SLAs, API integrations, and managed quality assurance.

enrichment.seo_title: Ravio Review 2026 - Compensation Benchmarking Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ravio is a real-time compensation benchmarking platform for tech companies, covering 46+ countries. See 2026 features, funding, and pricing notes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ravio is a London-based compensation intelligence platform founded in 2022 to modernize how fast-growing technology companies benchmark salary and equity data. Rather than relying on static, once-a-year compensation surveys, Ravio built a real-time data model: client companies connect their HR information systems (HRIS) to Ravio in a give-to-get arrangement, contributing anonymized compensation data in exchange for access to continuously updated market benchmarks. The platform reports coverage across more than 46 countries and 100-plus roles, which is particularly relevant for European and international tech companies that need pay data spanning multiple labor markets rather than a single country's averages. Ravio's co-CEOs are Roy Blanga and Merten Wulfert, both previously managers at Deliveroo, alongside additional co-founders; the company has scaled its headcount into the tens to roughly one hundred employees depending on the data source and reporting date. In May 2025, Ravio announced a 12 million dollar Series A funding round led by US-based Spark Capital, with participation from Blackbird (Australia) and Cherry Ventures (Europe), funding that the company said would go toward expanding its live benchmarking data and product further. Beyond raw benchmarking data, Ravio's platform includes tools for building compensation bands and pay architecture, running pay equity analysis to identify unexplained pay gaps, generating total rewards statements for employees, and providing reporting and analytics dashboards for people and total rewards teams. Because Ravio's compensation data is refreshed through live HRIS integrations rather than periodic manual surveys, the company positions this as more accurate and timely than legacy compensation survey providers, particularly for fast-scaling companies where roles, levels, and pay bands change frequently. Ravio does not publish self-serve pricing; access requires contacting the company's sales team, and pricing is understood to scale with company size, the modules licensed (benchmarking, banding, pay equity, and so on), and the number of HR system integrations required. The platform is best suited for People, Total Rewards, and HR leadership teams at venture-backed technology and growth-stage companies operating across multiple countries who need current market data to set competitive pay ranges, and for finance leaders who need defensible, up-to-date compensation benchmarks for budgeting and board reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ravio is a London-headquartered compensation benchmarking platform founded in 2022 to bring real-time, continuously updated pay data to fast-growing technology companies, replacing the static annual compensation surveys that have traditionally dominated the category.

The company is led by co-CEOs Roy Blanga and Merten Wulfert, both formerly managers at Deliveroo, and in May 2025 it raised a 12 million dollar Series A round led by Spark Capital, with Blackbird and Cherry Ventures also participating, to expand its live benchmarking data and product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ravio's core product is real-time compensation and equity benchmarking, covering more than 46 countries and 100-plus roles, sourced through a give-to-get model where client companies contribute anonymized HRIS data in exchange for market access. This structure is designed to keep benchmarks continuously current rather than refreshed only once a year.

Beyond raw benchmarks, the platform includes tools for building compensation bands and pay architecture, running pay equity analysis, generating employee-facing total rewards statements, and reporting dashboards, giving People and Total Rewards teams a fuller compensation management workflow rather than just a data lookup tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ravio does not publish pricing on its website; access to the platform requires contacting its sales team for a custom quote. Pricing is understood to scale with company size and the specific modules licensed, such as benchmarking, banding, or pay equity analysis.
As an enterprise SaaS product aimed at People and Total Rewards teams, Ravio is typically sold on annual contracts, consistent with other compensation intelligence platforms in its category.

features.title: Real-time compensation benchmarking

features.title: 46+ country and 100+ role coverage
features.description: Market data spanning a wide range of international labor markets relevant to global tech teams.

features.description: Identifies unexplained pay gaps across demographic groups to support fair-pay reporting.

features.title: Compensation bands and architecture builder
features.description: Tools for designing and maintaining structured pay bands and leveling frameworks.

features.title: HRIS integrations
features.description: Connects to client HR systems to keep benchmarking data continuously synced and current.

features.title: Total rewards statements
features.description: Generates employee-facing summaries of total compensation, including equity and benefits.

features.title: Reporting and analytics dashboards
features.description: Visualizes compensation data and trends for People and Total Rewards leadership.

pros: Real-time data model is more current than traditional annual compensation surveys
Broad international coverage across 46+ countries suits multi-country tech companies
Give-to-get data model incentivizes clients to keep their contributed data fresh
Backed by well-known venture investors including Spark Capital, Blackbird, and Cherry Ventures
Combines benchmarking with pay banding and pay equity tools in one platform
cons: Pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation to obtain a quote
Primarily built for tech and growth-stage companies, less tailored to other industries
Founded in 2022, giving it a shorter track record than legacy compensation survey providers
Benchmark data quality depends on the breadth and accuracy of participating clients' HRIS data

faqs.question: What does Ravio do?
faqs.answer: Ravio provides real-time compensation and equity benchmarking data, along with pay banding and pay equity analysis tools, primarily for technology companies.

faqs.question: When was Ravio founded?
faqs.answer: Ravio was founded in 2022 and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: How much funding has Ravio raised?
faqs.answer: Ravio raised a 12 million dollar Series A round in May 2025, led by Spark Capital with participation from Blackbird and Cherry Ventures.

faqs.question: Which countries does Ravio cover?
faqs.answer: Ravio reports compensation benchmarking coverage across more than 46 countries and 100-plus roles.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source distributed computing framework for scaling Python and AI/ML workloads from a laptop to a large GPU cluster
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (Apache 2.0 license); Anyscale sells a separate paid managed platform built on Ray
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); Anyscale's managed platform pricing is usage-based and not publicly listed
enrichment.company_size: Ray is an open-source project with 1,000+ contributors; Anyscale, the company commercializing Ray, was founded in 2019 and has not publicly disclosed headcount
enrichment.headquarters: Ray originated at UC Berkeley's RISELab; Anyscale, the company founded by Ray's creators, is headquartered in San Francisco, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Ray (ray.io) Review 2026 - Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ray is an open-source framework for scaling Python and AI workloads from a laptop to a cluster. See features, licensing, Anyscale pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ray is an open-source, Apache 2.0 licensed distributed computing framework designed to let developers scale Python and AI/ML applications from a single laptop up to clusters with thousands of GPUs or CPUs. The project originated at UC Berkeley's RISELab, the successor lab to the AMPLab that produced Apache Spark, and was created by researchers including Robert Nishihara, Philipp Moritz, and lab director Ion Stoica. In 2019, the same trio founded Anyscale, a company built to commercialize Ray with a fully managed platform, raising a 20.6 million dollar Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz shortly after launch. Anyscale is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and continues to lead core development of the open-source Ray project while selling a separate paid managed offering that adds enterprise governance, developer tooling, and expert support on top of the free, self-hosted framework. Ray itself is free to download and run anywhere; only Anyscale's managed cloud platform is a commercial product, and its pricing is usage-based and quoted individually rather than published as a fixed price list. Architecturally, Ray Core provides low-level primitives (tasks, actors, and distributed objects) that let developers parallelize ordinary Python code with minimal changes, while a cluster-based architecture with a head node and worker nodes handles scheduling, resource allocation, and fault tolerance automatically. On top of Ray Core, the project ships a set of higher-level AI libraries: Ray Data for distributed, multi-modal data processing; Ray Train for distributed model training; Ray Tune for hyperparameter optimization; Ray Serve for scalable model deployment and serving, including large language model serving; and RLlib for reinforcement learning. This combination lets a single framework cover most stages of a modern AI/ML pipeline, from data preprocessing through training, tuning, and production serving, rather than requiring separate tools stitched together for each stage. Ray has built one of the larger open-source communities in the distributed AI infrastructure space, with the project reporting more than 34,800 GitHub stars, over 40,000 repository downloads, and 1,000-plus contributors, and it is used in production by major technology companies including reported usage in training and scaling infrastructure associated with OpenAI's ChatGPT, along with adoption at companies such as Amazon, Microsoft, Ant Financial, and Intel. Ray is primarily a Python-native framework, though Ray Serve also exposes a Java API for cross-language model serving in polyglot environments. Because Ray requires familiarity with distributed systems concepts and cluster configuration, it carries a steeper learning curve than single-machine tools, and while the open-source project is free, teams that want managed infrastructure, autoscaling across clouds, and dedicated support typically turn to Anyscale's commercial platform rather than operating Ray clusters entirely themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ray is an open-source distributed computing framework, developed at UC Berkeley's RISELab, that lets Python and AI/ML workloads scale from a single laptop to a cluster spanning thousands of GPUs or CPUs. It is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is free to use and self-host.

The framework was commercialized starting in 2019 when its creators, Robert Nishihara, Philipp Moritz, and Ion Stoica, founded Anyscale, a San Francisco-based company that leads Ray's ongoing open-source development while also selling a separate managed cloud platform built on top of it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ray Core provides distributed computing primitives, tasks, actors, and objects, that let developers parallelize Python code with minimal modification, backed by an automatically managed cluster architecture with a head node coordinating scheduling and worker nodes executing tasks.

On top of Ray Core, purpose-built AI libraries cover distributed data processing (Ray Data), model training (Ray Train), hyperparameter tuning (Ray Tune), model serving including LLM serving (Ray Serve), and reinforcement learning (RLlib), making Ray a single framework capable of handling most stages of a machine learning pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The open-source Ray framework itself is completely free under the Apache 2.0 license, and organizations can self-host and scale Ray clusters on their own infrastructure or any major cloud provider at no licensing cost.

Anyscale, the company founded by Ray's original creators, sells a separate managed platform that adds enterprise governance, autoscaling, developer tooling, and support. Anyscale's pricing is usage-based and not published as a fixed rate card; prospective customers need to contact Anyscale directly for a quote.

features.title: Ray Core primitives
features.description: Tasks, actors, and distributed objects that let developers parallelize Python applications with minimal code changes.

features.title: Ray Data
features.description: Distributed, multi-modal data processing library for large-scale ETL and preprocessing pipelines.

features.title: Ray Train
features.description: Distributed model training library supporting popular ML frameworks across multi-node GPU clusters.

features.title: Ray Tune
features.description: Scalable hyperparameter optimization library for tuning models across many parallel trials.

features.title: Ray Serve
features.description: Model serving library for deploying and scaling machine learning models and LLM inference in production.

features.title: RLlib
features.description: Reinforcement learning library built on Ray for scalable RL training and research.

features.title: Autoscaling cluster management
features.description: Automatically provisions and scales cluster resources based on workload demand.

features.title: Multi-cloud and Kubernetes support
features.description: Deployable across major cloud providers and via Kubernetes using the KubeRay project.

pros: Free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Scales seamlessly from a single laptop to clusters with thousands of nodes
Used in production by major AI companies, including reported use in ChatGPT-related infrastructure
Comprehensive library ecosystem covering data, training, tuning, and serving in one framework
Large, active open-source community with 1,000+ contributors and 34,800+ GitHub stars
cons: Distributed systems concepts add a learning curve compared to single-machine Python tools
Self-managing Ray clusters without Anyscale requires nontrivial DevOps expertise
Debugging failures across a distributed cluster can be more difficult than single-node debugging
Enterprise-grade support and managed infrastructure are only available through the separately priced Anyscale platform

faqs.question: What is Ray?
faqs.answer: Ray is an open-source framework for scaling Python and AI applications from a single laptop to a distributed cluster, developed originally at UC Berkeley's RISELab.

faqs.question: Is Ray free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ray is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license. Anyscale sells a separate paid managed platform built on top of it.

faqs.question: Who created Ray?
faqs.answer: Ray was created by researchers Robert Nishihara, Philipp Moritz, and Ion Stoica at UC Berkeley's RISELab; the same team founded Anyscale in 2019 to commercialize it.

faqs.question: What is Anyscale's relationship to Ray?
faqs.answer: Anyscale is the company founded by Ray's creators. It leads Ray's open-source development and sells a separate managed enterprise platform built on the open-source framework.

faqs.question: What is Ray used for?
faqs.answer: Ray is used for distributed model training, hyperparameter tuning, batch inference, model and LLM serving, reinforcement learning, and general distributed Python computation.

faqs.question: Is Ray connected to OpenAI's ChatGPT?
faqs.answer: Public reporting has described Ray being used in infrastructure associated with training and scaling systems related to OpenAI's ChatGPT.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Ray support?
faqs.answer: Ray is primarily Python-native; Ray Serve also offers a Java API for cross-language model serving.

faqs.question: How large is the Ray open-source community?
faqs.answer: As reported on ray.io, the project has more than 34,800 GitHub stars, over 40,000 downloads, and 1,000-plus contributors.

enrichment.short_description: Application performance monitoring, crash reporting, and real user monitoring platform for web and mobile apps
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based subscription tiers sold per product (Crash Reporting, APM, Real User Monitoring), with an annual billing discount
enrichment.starting_price: From $40/month (billed annually) for the Crash Reporting Basic plan, covering up to 100,000 errors per month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (mid-size private company with offices in Wellington, New Zealand and Seattle, United States)
enrichment.headquarters: Wellington, New Zealand, with an additional office in Seattle, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Raygun Review 2026 - APM and Crash Reporting Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Raygun is an application performance monitoring and crash reporting platform. See 2026 pricing for Crash Reporting, APM, and Real User Monitoring plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Raygun is an application performance monitoring, crash reporting, and real user monitoring platform founded by John-Daniel Trask and Jeremy Boyd, who started their original company (originally called Mindscape, alongside co-founder Andrew Peters) in Wellington, New Zealand in 2007. The Raygun product name and brand were adopted in 2013 when the founders decided their developer tools business needed a more memorable identity, and the company has operated as Raygun since. Raygun is headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, with an additional office in Seattle, United States, reflecting its dual presence in the Australasian and North American markets. The platform's core purpose is to help software teams detect, diagnose, and resolve issues in production applications by combining three connected products: Crash Reporting, which pinpoints the root cause of errors and intelligently prioritizes them by the number of affected users rather than raw error count, supports virtually any language or framework via lightweight SDKs, and tracks both client-side and server-side errors across desktop and mobile apps; Application Performance Monitoring (APM), which provides server-side performance visibility through visual flame charts showing full trace capture with method-level timing precision, identifying exactly which functions ran, how long they took, and where a slow request originated; and Real User Monitoring (RUM), which tracks actual front-end performance across real user sessions, offering waterfall timeline visualizations, Core Web Vitals and UX metrics, and the ability to filter performance data by browser, geography, device, or application version, and to correlate user experience with engineering events like deployments. Raygun sells these three products on separate usage-based pricing tracks, each scaling by monthly volume, errors for Crash Reporting, sessions for Real User Monitoring, and traces for APM, with tiers named Basic, Team, Business, and a custom Enterprise tier. Annual billing offers roughly a third off the equivalent monthly rate across all three products, and every tier includes unlimited applications, unlimited team members, and a 14-day free trial. Higher tiers add features like inbound filters and third-party integrations at the Team level, and SAML single sign-on plus dedicated account management at the Business level. Raygun is commonly used by software development and DevOps teams that need to combine error tracking with performance diagnostics in a single platform rather than operating separate crash-reporting and APM tools, and it is positioned as a competitor to tools such as Sentry, New Relic, and Datadog in different parts of its product line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Raygun traces its roots to 2007, when founders John-Daniel Trask and Jeremy Boyd started a Wellington, New Zealand-based developer tools business (originally named Mindscape). The Raygun product name and brand were adopted in 2013, and the company has since focused on application error tracking and performance monitoring.

Raygun is headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, with an additional office in Seattle, United States, and its platform now spans three connected products: Crash Reporting, Application Performance Monitoring (APM), and Real User Monitoring (RUM), aimed at software teams that want error tracking and performance diagnostics in one place.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Crash Reporting pinpoints root causes of application errors, prioritizes issues by the number of affected users, and supports virtually any language or framework through lightweight SDKs across desktop and mobile. APM adds server-side visibility through visual flame charts with method-level timing precision, showing exactly which functions ran and how long each took.

Real User Monitoring tracks actual front-end performance across real user sessions with waterfall timeline visualizations, Core Web Vitals and UX metrics, and filtering by browser, geography, device, and application version, letting teams correlate user experience issues with specific deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Raygun prices Crash Reporting, APM, and Real User Monitoring separately, each on usage-based tiers, Basic, Team, Business, and Enterprise, that scale with monthly volume of errors, traces, or sessions respectively. Annual billing saves roughly a third compared to monthly billing across all three products.

Every tier includes unlimited applications and unlimited team members, with a 14-day free trial available. Higher tiers unlock features such as inbound filters, third-party integrations, SAML single sign-on, and dedicated account management, and additional usage beyond a plan's quota is billed incrementally.

pros: Combines crash reporting, APM, and real user monitoring in one connected platform
Prioritizes errors by number of affected users, helping teams focus on high-impact issues
Supports a broad range of languages and frameworks via lightweight SDKs
All plans include unlimited apps and team members regardless of tier
Straightforward usage-based pricing with a meaningful annual billing discount
cons: Crash Reporting, APM, and RUM are priced and purchased as separate products
Usage overages beyond a plan's included volume add to monthly cost
Enterprise-level features like SAML SSO require the highest paid tier
Company financial and headcount details are not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: What does Raygun do?
faqs.answer: Raygun provides crash reporting, application performance monitoring, and real user monitoring to help software teams detect, diagnose, and resolve issues in production applications.

faqs.question: When was Raygun founded?
faqs.answer: Raygun's founding company, originally named Mindscape, was started in Wellington, New Zealand in 2007; the Raygun product and brand were introduced in 2013.

faqs.question: Where is Raygun headquartered?
faqs.answer: Raygun is headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, with an additional office in Seattle, United States.

faqs.question: How is Raygun priced?
faqs.answer: Raygun sells Crash Reporting, APM, and Real User Monitoring separately, each on usage-based tiers (Basic, Team, Business, Enterprise) scaled by monthly errors, traces, or sessions.

faqs.question: Does Raygun offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Raygun offers a 14-day free trial across its plans.

faqs.question: Does Raygun support mobile applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Crash Reporting tracks both client-side and server-side errors across desktop and mobile applications.

faqs.question: What are Core Web Vitals in Raygun's RUM product?
faqs.answer: Core Web Vitals are front-end performance metrics that Raygun's Real User Monitoring tracks as part of real user session data.

faqs.question: Does Raygun offer single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAML SSO is available on Raygun's Business-tier plans.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source command-line tool to sync, copy, mount, and manage files across 70+ cloud storage providers
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (MIT license); no paid tiers
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable - open-source project maintained by a volunteer community, led by original creator Nick Craig-Wood
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (open-source project with no corporate headquarters); primary maintainer based in the United Kingdom
enrichment.seo_title: Rclone Review 2026 - Features, Supported Providers and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rclone is a free, open-source command-line tool for syncing and managing files across 70+ cloud storage providers. See features, use cases, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rclone is a free, open-source command-line program for managing and synchronizing files with cloud storage, often described by its own community as the Swiss Army knife of cloud storage. The project began with its first code commit on November 18, 2012, written by Nick Craig-Wood, who has remained its primary creator and maintainer since. Rclone is written in the Go programming language and distributed under the MIT license, meaning it is free to use, modify, and redistribute, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or corporate ownership; it is community-maintained rather than backed by a company, and is available through standard package managers including apt (Ubuntu/Debian), Fedora's package repository, Homebrew, and Chocolatey. Functionally, Rclone provides cloud-storage equivalents of familiar Unix commands such as rsync, cp, mv, mount, and ls, letting users copy, synchronize, and move files between local storage and cloud providers, or between two different cloud providers directly. It supports more than 70 cloud storage products and protocols, including major services such as Amazon S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, Backblaze B2, Box, Azure Blob and Azure Files, and standard protocols like WebDAV, FTP, and SFTP. Beyond simple copying, Rclone supports several distinct transfer modes: one-way sync, two-way synchronization via its bisync command, and move operations, all with continuous checksum verification and the ability to resume interrupted transfers from the last successfully completed file rather than starting over. It can also mount cloud storage as a local network drive on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD, and can serve files over SFTP, HTTP, WebDAV, FTP, and DLNA. Virtual backend layers add capabilities like client-side encryption (crypt), compression, chunking, and combining multiple remotes into a union, all applied transparently on top of an underlying cloud connection. The project has built a large open-source community, with its GitHub repository reporting tens of thousands of stars and active ongoing development, including a March 2021 release (1.55) that added features sponsored by CERN's EU-funded CS3MESH4EOSC project to support vendor-neutral cloud storage sharing for academic and research institutions. Because it is entirely command-line driven with no official graphical interface (though third-party GUIs exist), Rclone is most commonly used by IT administrators migrating large datasets between cloud providers, developers automating cloud backups through cron jobs or scheduled tasks, and privacy-conscious users who want to encrypt files locally before uploading them to a cloud provider, rather than by non-technical end users looking for a point-and-click sync app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rclone is a free, open-source command-line tool for managing and synchronizing files with cloud storage, created by Nick Craig-Wood with its first commit dated November 18, 2012. Written in Go and released under the MIT license, it has no corporate ownership and is maintained by its open-source community.

The project is often described as the Swiss Army knife of cloud storage because it replicates familiar Unix file commands, rsync, cp, mv, mount, and ls, against cloud backends, letting users work with remote storage the same way they would work with a local filesystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rclone supports more than 70 cloud storage providers and protocols, including Amazon S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, Backblaze B2, and Azure Blob and Azure Files, alongside standard protocols like WebDAV, FTP, and SFTP. It offers multiple transfer modes, one-way sync, two-way sync via bisync, copy, and move, with continuous checksum verification and the ability to resume interrupted transfers.

Additional capabilities include mounting cloud storage as a local drive on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD; serving files over SFTP, HTTP, WebDAV, and DLNA; and virtual backend layers for client-side encryption, compression, and chunking that can be applied transparently on top of any supported cloud connection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rclone is completely free and open source under the MIT license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or usage limits imposed by the project itself; costs, if any, come only from the cloud storage providers a user connects to rclone.

Because it is community-maintained rather than sold by a company, there is no official paid support tier or enterprise contract; users rely on community documentation, forums, and the GitHub issue tracker for support.

features.title: 70+ cloud storage backends
features.description: Native support for major providers including Amazon S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, Backblaze B2, and Azure, plus standard protocols like WebDAV and SFTP.

features.title: Sync, copy, move, and bisync
features.description: Multiple transfer modes including one-way sync, direct copy/move, and two-way (bidirectional) synchronization.

features.title: Resumable, checksum-verified transfers
features.description: Continuously verifies data integrity and can resume interrupted transfers from the last successfully completed file.

features.title: Mount cloud storage as a local drive
features.description: Presents remote cloud storage as a mounted filesystem on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD.

features.title: Serve files over standard protocols
features.description: Can serve cloud-backed files over SFTP, HTTP, WebDAV, FTP, and DLNA.

features.title: Client-side encryption (crypt backend)
features.description: Encrypts files locally before they are uploaded to a cloud provider, keeping data private even from the storage host.

features.title: Cross-cloud transfers
features.description: Moves or syncs files directly between two different cloud storage providers without a local intermediate copy.

features.title: Bandwidth throttling and scheduling
features.description: Allows limiting transfer speed and scheduling sync jobs for automated, unattended operation.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Extremely broad cloud provider support with 70+ backends
Cross-platform, running on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD
Actively maintained since 2012 with frequent releases
Powerful scripting and automation via a full-featured command-line interface
cons: Command-line only by default, with a steeper learning curve for non-technical users
No official vendor support or SLA since it is community-maintained
Advanced features like crypt, union, and bisync require careful configuration to avoid mistakes
Third-party GUIs exist but are not officially maintained by the core rclone project

faqs.question: Is Rclone free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rclone is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What cloud storage providers does Rclone support?
faqs.answer: Rclone supports more than 70 cloud storage products and protocols, including Amazon S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, Backblaze B2, and Azure.

faqs.question: Who created Rclone?
faqs.answer: Rclone was created by Nick Craig-Wood, with its first code commit dated November 18, 2012.

faqs.question: Can Rclone sync files in both directions?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rclone's bisync command supports two-way synchronization between a local folder and a cloud remote, or between two cloud remotes.

faqs.question: Does Rclone have a graphical user interface?
faqs.answer: Not officially; Rclone is primarily a command-line tool, though community-built GUIs such as RcloneView exist.

faqs.question: Can I mount cloud storage as a drive with Rclone?
faqs.answer: Yes, the rclone mount command presents cloud storage as a local network drive on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD.

faqs.question: Is Rclone secure?
faqs.answer: Rclone supports client-side encryption through its crypt backend and continuously verifies file checksums during transfers.

faqs.question: Can Rclone be automated for scheduled backups?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rclone is commonly run via cron jobs or Task Scheduler to automate recurring cloud backup and sync jobs.

use_cases.title: Cloud-to-cloud data migration
use_cases.description: IT administrators use Rclone to migrate large volumes of files directly between cloud storage providers without a local intermediate copy.

use_cases.title: Automated cloud backups
use_cases.description: Developers and power users script Rclone with cron or Task Scheduler to run recurring encrypted backups to cloud storage.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused cloud storage
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious users encrypt files locally with Rclone's crypt backend before uploading them to a cloud provider.

features: Community support via docs, forums, and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered customer service helpdesk and live chat platform built for ecommerce and online businesses
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-user monthly subscription tiers, plus a flat-rate/volume-based plan option, with an annual billing discount
enrichment.starting_price: $29/user/month (billed monthly), or $59/month flat-rate Starter plan for unlimited users capped at 500 conversations/month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed; operates under GoDaddy ownership per current company listings
enrichment.seo_title: Re:amaze Review 2026 - Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Re:amaze is an AI-powered helpdesk and live chat platform for ecommerce businesses. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Re:amaze is a customer service helpdesk and live chat platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Jose, California, built specifically for ecommerce and online businesses. Current company listings show Re:amaze operating under GoDaddy, indicating it has become part of GoDaddy's business software portfolio, though it continues to be marketed and sold as Re:amaze. The platform positions itself as an AI-powered customer service and AI agent platform, unifying multiple support channels, email, live chat, social media, mobile SMS, VoIP calling, video calls, and push notifications, into a single shared inbox so support teams can manage every customer conversation from one place regardless of channel. Verified customers referenced in Re:amaze's own marketing include Printful, a print-on-demand ecommerce company, BuiltBar, a food and beverage ecommerce brand, and Moviepilot, a media platform serving more than 30 million users, illustrating its use both in direct ecommerce and in content/media businesses with high support volume. Re:amaze's product includes a Re:amaze AI Agent that automates common customer resolutions, workflow automation and chatbot tools for routing and answering routine questions, a public and internal FAQ knowledge base, and, on higher tiers, features like multi-brand management for companies running several storefronts under one support team, live visitor view for watching real-time site activity, in-chat screensharing through a feature called Peek, department and staff shift management, custom staff roles, and customer satisfaction ratings. Pricing is structured per team member across three named tiers, Basic at 29 dollars per user per month, Pro at 49 dollars per user per month, and Plus at 69 dollars per user per month, each roughly 10 percent cheaper when billed annually, plus a flat-rate Starter plan at 59 dollars per month that includes Basic-tier features for an unlimited number of team members but caps usage at 500 resolved conversations per month. The Basic and Plus tiers each include a monthly allotment of AI-powered resolutions handled by the Re:amaze AI Agent (5 per user on Basic, 20 per user on Plus), with additional AI resolutions beyond that allotment billed at 85 cents each. All plans include a 14-day free trial that unlocks Plus-tier features for evaluation, and Re:amaze advertises no long-term contracts, allowing month-to-month billing and cancellation. Re:amaze is widely distributed through the Shopify App Store as a helpdesk and live chat app, reflecting its strong focus on ecommerce merchants, and it competes with other ecommerce-focused support tools such as Gorgias. It is best suited for online retailers and ecommerce brands, particularly Shopify merchants, that need to centralize support across chat, email, and social channels and want built-in AI automation for routine customer questions, rather than for large enterprises with highly complex, multi-department support operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Re:amaze is a customer service helpdesk and live chat platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Jose, California, built specifically for ecommerce and online businesses. Current company listings show it operating under GoDaddy while continuing to be marketed as Re:amaze.

The platform unifies email, live chat, social media, SMS, VoIP calling, video calls, and push notifications into a single shared inbox, positioning itself as an AI-powered customer service and AI agent platform for support teams handling high conversation volume across multiple channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include a Re:amaze AI Agent that automates common customer resolutions, workflow automation and chatbots for routing routine questions, and a combined public and internal FAQ knowledge base. Higher tiers add multi-brand management, live visitor view, in-chat screensharing (Peek), department and shift management, and customer satisfaction ratings.

Re:amaze is heavily distributed through the Shopify App Store, reflecting deep integration with ecommerce workflows, and is used by both direct-to-consumer brands and high-traffic media platforms that need to manage large volumes of customer conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Re:amaze prices its main plans per team member: Basic at 29 dollars per user per month, Pro at 49 dollars, and Plus at 69 dollars, each with roughly a 10 percent discount when billed annually. A flat-rate Starter plan at 59 dollars per month covers Basic-tier features for unlimited team members but caps usage at 500 conversations per month.

Basic and Plus plans include a set number of monthly AI-powered resolutions per user (5 on Basic, 20 on Plus) handled by the Re:amaze AI Agent, with additional AI resolutions billed at 85 cents each. All plans include a 14-day free trial with Plus-tier features and no long-term contract requirement.

features.title: Unified omnichannel inbox
features.description: Combines email, live chat, social media, SMS, VoIP, and video calls into a single shared support inbox.

features.title: Re:amaze AI Agent
features.description: AI-powered automation that resolves common customer questions without human agent involvement, included with monthly quotas per plan.

features.title: Workflow automation and chatbots
features.description: Rules-based automation and chatbot flows for routing and answering routine customer inquiries.

features.title: Public and internal FAQ knowledge base
features.description: Self-service help center for customers plus an internal knowledge base for support staff.

features.title: Multi-brand management
features.description: Manage support for multiple storefronts or brands from a single Re:amaze account on higher tiers.

features.title: Live visitor view and Peek screensharing
features.description: Monitor real-time site visitor activity and offer in-chat screensharing for hands-on troubleshooting.

features.title: Shopify App Store integration
features.description: Deep integration with Shopify for order status, customer data, and ecommerce-specific support workflows.

features.title: Customer satisfaction ratings and reporting
features.description: Collects post-conversation satisfaction ratings and provides staff performance and advanced reporting on higher tiers.

pros: Combines email, chat, social, SMS, and voice channels in one unified inbox
Purpose-built for ecommerce workflows with strong Shopify integration
Includes built-in AI Agent for automating common customer resolutions
Flat-rate Starter plan offers unlimited team members at a fixed monthly cost
Month-to-month billing with no long-term contract required
cons: AI resolutions beyond the monthly quota are billed per resolution, adding variable cost
Advanced reporting, multi-brand management, and screensharing are reserved for higher tiers
Feature set is less extensive than some enterprise-focused helpdesk platforms for complex support orgs
Now operating under GoDaddy ownership, which may affect long-term independent product direction

faqs.question: What is Re:amaze used for?
faqs.answer: Re:amaze is a customer service helpdesk and live chat platform used by ecommerce and online businesses to manage support across email, chat, social, SMS, and voice from one inbox.

faqs.question: Does Re:amaze integrate with Shopify?
faqs.answer: Yes, Re:amaze is available as a Shopify App Store app and is widely used by Shopify merchants for helpdesk and live chat support.

faqs.question: Does Re:amaze offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Re:amaze offers a 14-day free trial that includes Plus-tier features.

faqs.question: What is the Starter plan?
faqs.answer: The Starter plan is a 59 dollar per month flat-rate option that includes Basic-tier features for unlimited team members, capped at 500 conversations per month.

faqs.question: Does Re:amaze support AI-powered customer responses?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Re:amaze AI Agent automates common customer resolutions, with a monthly quota included per plan and per-resolution billing beyond that quota.

faqs.question: Can Re:amaze handle SMS and voice support?
faqs.answer: Yes, SMS and voice channels are available starting on the Pro plan and above.

faqs.question: Who owns Re:amaze?
faqs.answer: Re:amaze currently operates under GoDaddy according to current company listings, while continuing to be marketed under the Re:amaze brand.

faqs.question: When was Re:amaze founded?
faqs.answer: Re:amaze was founded in 2012 and is headquartered in San Jose, California.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce customer support centralization
use_cases.description: Online retailers use Re:amaze to manage chat, email, and social media support from one shared inbox across their storefront.

use_cases.title: Shopify merchant support automation
use_cases.description: Shopify store owners use Re:amaze's AI Agent and chatbots to automatically answer routine order-status and FAQ questions.

use_cases.title: High-volume support with live troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Support teams handling high conversation volume use live visitor view and Peek screensharing to troubleshoot customer issues in real time.
use_cases.audience: Customer support teams at high-traffic online businesses

features: Unlimited email inboxes, live chat, social channels
